coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/carboxymethyl starch bio-coated naproxen@GQDs/Copper glutamate MOFs: A new system for colon-specific drug delivery relay on the special structure of the used polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Hassanpouraghdam, Yashar
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: In the present investigation, for the first time, bio metal–organic frameworks (Bio-MOFs) were synthesized with glutamic acid (Glu) as a biocompatible organic linker and copper as a metal node in presence of presynthesized citric acid-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)). SEM analysis obtained a diameter of Bio-MOF(Cu) around 1 μm. Naproxen (NAP) as a model of a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug was loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) at about 53.1 %. NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) showed fast drug release, hence, for achieving the system with controlled and delayed intestinal delivery the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was coated with carboxymethyl starch (CMS) and chitosan (CS). The physicochemical properties of the products were evaluated employing well-known characterization techniques. NAP release results displayed that the CMS@CS can afford gastric protection and is a more suitable drug carrier for colon targeting. Also, the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) showed biodegradability (68.7 %), biocompatibility (78.3 %), and antibacterial (75 µg·mL−1) features. Consequently, the finding displayed that the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) potentially could be employed for encapsulation, protection, and delivery of NAP in pharmaceutical applications without any concern about bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carboxymethyl starch
Chitosan
Ionotropic gelation
Bio-metal organic frameworks
Graphene quantum dots
Naproxen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for loading of NAP on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) nanohybrid
Coating of the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) with CMS and CS in core–shell bead form
Study the swelling behavior of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) core–shell hydrogel beads

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The general schematization of the used protocol for the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) synthesis.
FT-IR spectra of GQDs, Bio-MOF(Cu), GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), NAP, NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) (a), CMS, CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) (b).
XRD spectra of GQDs, Bio-MOF(Cu), GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)).
BET-plot (a), N2 adsorption/desorption (b), and BJH-plot of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) (c).
Photoluminescence characteristics at an excitation energy of 365 nm.
SEM image of GQDs (a), Bio-MOF(Cu) (b), GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) (c,d), and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) (e,f).
Surface characterizations of GQDs (a) and GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) (b) employing AFM images.

Degradation behavior (a), swelling pattern (b), comparison of the in vitro NAP release pattern from NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) at simulated GIT condition (c), and proposed schematic way of the NAP oral delivery with CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) (d).
MTT assay results on HT 29 cells after 8 h incubation with NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)). Results are expressed as mean ± S.D. of three experiments.

head.ce:title: Chitosan/carboxymethyl starch bio-coated naproxen@GQDs/Copper glutamate MOFs: A new system for colon-specific drug delivery relay on the special structure of the used polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present investigation, for the first time, bio metal–organic frameworks (Bio-MOFs) were synthesized with glutamic acid (Glu) as a biocompatible organic linker and copper as a metal node in presence of presynthesized citric acid-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)). SEM analysis obtained a diameter of Bio-MOF(Cu) around 1 μm. Naproxen (NAP) as a model of a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug was loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) at about 53.1 %. NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) showed fast drug release, hence, for achieving the system with controlled and delayed intestinal delivery the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was coated with carboxymethyl starch (CMS) and chitosan (CS). The physicochemical properties of the products were evaluated employing well-known characterization techniques. NAP release results displayed that the CMS@CS can afford gastric protection and is a more suitable drug carrier for colon targeting. Also, the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) showed biodegradability (68.7 %), biocompatibility (78.3 %), and antibacterial (75 µg·mL−1) features. Consequently, the finding displayed that the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) potentially could be employed for encapsulation, protection, and delivery of NAP in pharmaceutical applications without any concern about bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Achieving a better dissolution and bioavailability of poorly water-soluble drugs is one of the main demands in pharmaceuticals. Over the years, many kinds of materials have been designed and developed as new challenging therapeutic agent carriers to solve the drawbacks associated with them [1–5]. NAP is a propionic acid derivative with a chemical formula of C14H14O3Na. This member of the nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs family has been used to treat musculoskeletal, arthritis, headache, and post-operative pain, as well as fever via nonselective cyclooxygenase inhibition [6,7]. However, gastrointestinal side effects associated with the oral administration and poor aqueous solubility (0.025 mg/mL at 25 ◦C) are two main drawbacks of it. Therefore, traditional oral delivery of NAP needs to be managed [8]. Interestingly, nanostructures can eliminate various challenges in medical science owing to their unique properties [9–12]. It is reported that drug loading into nanoporous materials is one of the efficient attempts to solve this poor solubility problem [13]. Of several porous nanomaterials, MOFs as a new class of nanoporous crystalline inorganic–organic hybrid materials are increasingly favorable for improving drug delivery performance because of their large specific surface area, high drug loading capacity, devisable composition, the possibility of their functionality, etc. [14–20]. In this between, Bio-MOFs are a class of MOFs that in their structure biomolecules are used as a homogeneous metal phase coupling agent or coordinated ligands [9]. Amino acids like Glu is one of the recently proposed biomolecule-based linker for Bio-MOFs synthesis. Nevertheless, the easy agglomeration of MOFs is one serious problem that made them less effective for practical applications. In this context, the synthesis of MOFs in the presence of host substrates is imperative to address this intractable issue and retain active surface sites and monodispersity [21,22]. Among all other materials, recently, GQDs with numerous oxygen-containing functionalities have been prompted because of the oxygen-containing functional groups that enabled them to interact with other materials [23–27]. GQDs have the favorable properties of water solubility, appropriate photo-stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and notable chemical, and optical properties [28]. Consequently, combining the specific characteristics of MOFs with GQDs for the preparation of GQDs@MOF is a promising strategy to fabricate a new generation of hybrid materials. Despite the mentioned unique benefits, the use of pristine nanomaterials or nanohybrids is limited because of their possible fast degradation in the human body's metabolic systems. Hence, the incorporation of them into stable coats like organic biopolymers can reduce their degradation rate and improve their applicability [29,30]. Among various biopolymeric systems, carbohydrates have attracted much interest due to their outstanding advantages. Today the CMS with polyanionic property has received more interest owing to the presence of –CH2COO- on its structure [31–34]. The ionotropic gelation of this polysaccharide at room temperature and in an aqueous medium with the common crosslinking ions yields well-spherical-shaped beads. Moreover, the –CH2COO- groups are beneficial and can form a complex with polycations like CS [35]. In fact, CS as another polysaccharide can form complexes with CMS as a result of electrostatic interaction [30,36,37]. The formed electrostatic interaction between the CS and CMS produces a pH-sensitive polyelectrolyte complex by combining advantages of the individual CS and CMS, and also can allows customization of drug-release profile in the gastrointestinal tract [38–40].

By considering the above-reported facts, and in the following of our ongoing research project associated with the development of new controlled oral drug delivery agents based on GQDs@MOF [41,42] and polysaccharides [39,43,44] and with the aim of removing the problem of poor aqueous solubility of NAP, in the current research work, for the first time a new oral formulation has been established by considering the benefits of the GQDs, Bi-MOF(Cu), CMS, and CS to apply the synergy contributions of them for the efficient NAP oral delivery. Although there is some published research work on the MOF/poly saccharides-based drug carriers, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the fabrication CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) for colon-controlled delivery of NAP. Consequently, from the application and type of components viewpoints, the synthesized CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) is a new vehicle with having novelty. After structure confirmation, the proposed system displayed reliable results as verified by the in vitro experiments, and hence, can be proposed as a controlled NAP oral delivery vehicle.

Citric acid (CA), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Glu, copper(II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), triethylamine (TA), ethanol (EtOH), starch (Mw = 9500 g mol−1), CS with medium molecular weight (MW 50,000–190,000 Da and 80.3 % of deacetylation degree), isopropanol, monochloroacetic acid, glacial acetic acid, hydrogen chloride (HCl), bromophenol blue, acetone, and ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were procured from Merck. 2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich. Penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) were purchased from Gibco, USA. NAP (98 % purity) was provided by Danna Pharma Co. (Tabriz, Iran). All used chemicals and reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. HT 29 as a human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line was received (to do the MTT test on it as a colon cell line model) by the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran). Double distilled water was utilized overall in this research work.

Fourier transform infrared spectra of the samples were recorded using Bruker Instruments (FT-IR, model Aquinox 55, Germany) as KBr disks. The FT-IR measurement range and resolution were 400–4000 cm−1 and 4 cm−1 respectively. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 1700 UV–vis Spectrophotometer. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) tests were done on BET Sorptometer (BELsorp, Japan) at 77 K. The PHS-3CpH meter specified the pH of the used aqueous solutions. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were obtained on a Siemens diffractometer X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu Kα (1.54 Å) radiation and a scanning rate of 2°/min. The morphology and elemental composition of the dried samples were observed by TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope (SEM) after coating the samples with a thin layer of gold. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. The topology of the synthesized samples was evaluated by employing atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanosurf Mobile S in the tapping mode).

GQDs were synthesized according to the previously described procedure [45]. Typically, 2 g of CA was poured into a ceramic crucible and then heated at 200 °C in a furnace. After 30 min the orange liquid was obtained. In the following, the produced orange liquid was added to the aqueous solution of NaOH (10 mg/mL) to be neutral. The solvent of the obtained GQDs was eliminated under reduced pressure. Finally, the powder of GQDs was achieved after drying in a vacuum oven. The achieved GQDs were kept in the dark vial for the next use.

For the synthesis of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) nanohybrid, at first, in a two-necked ballon, the Glu (7.36 g, 0.05 mol) was dissolved in 10 mL of NaOH (0.1 mol) solution. Then the GQDs (30 %) was added to it. The mixture magnetically was stirred to dissolve. On the other hand, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (12.08 g, 0.05 mol) was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. In the next step, the copper solution was moved to the dropping funnel and slowly dropped into the aqueous solution of Glu/GQDs. At the same time, the TA was dropped into the reaction solution. After the complete addition of copper solution and TA, the reaction was continued overnight at ambient temperature. The resultant solid products were collected by centrifuging and washed with deionized water, weak acid, and water/EtOH (1:1) respectively until the pH value of the residue solution be ∼ 6.0. The obtained GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) nanohybrid was dried at 50 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven. Pristin Bio-MOF(Cu) was also prepared using the same procedure but in the absence of GQDs [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for loading of NAP on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para:
Anti-inflammatory NAP drug was taken as a model drug to estimate the load and release performance of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu). Initially, the NAP was added to EtOH (20 mL) and then stirred for 2 h to dissolve. Afterward, about 200 mg of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was added to the drug solution. The dispersion was shaken for 24 h on a shaker at room temperature. The NAP-loaded GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was separated from the free drug solution using a centrifuge and rinsed with EtOH to remove the un-loaded drug. The supernatant was analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer at λmax of NAP. Then, the un-loaded NAP content in the loading medium was calculated by an established NAP calibration curve. In the end, the NAP loading percentage is calculated by the below-described equations [48].
(1)
D r u g l o a d i n g c a p a c i t y
% w
=
MassofNAPincarrier
Massofnanocarrier
× 100
(2)
D r u g e n t r a p m e n t e f f i c i e n c y
% w
=
MassofNAPincarrier
MassofNAPfedinitially
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) with CMS and CS in core–shell bead form

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CMS was performed based on the published report [49] with minor revisions. Initially, the NaOH (2.4 g) was added to 44 mL of H2O/isopropanol (15:85) and the reaction mixture was stirred to dissolve. Then the starch was added gradually to the reaction flask. The temperature of the reaction was adjusted to 60 °C. Monochloroacetic acid (3 g) was added to the reaction mixture for 30 min. The reaction was followed for another 2 h under this condition. After this time, the resultant was cooled to room temperature, the solvent was decanted, and the produced CMS was suspended in aqueous methanol. After overnight the gained precipitate was neutralized with 10 %v/v of acetic acid and several times was washed with methanol. Finally, a white powder of CMS was obtained after vacuum drying at 60 °C. The degree of substitution (DS) of the synthesized CMS was calculated at about 0.44 (DS = 0.44).

For the preparation of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) hydrogel beads, at first, CMS solution was prepared through the dissolving of CMS (1 g) in deionized water (7 mL). After the complete dissolving of CMS, the suspension of NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) (3 mL, 20 %w/w) was added to the CMS solution. The mixture was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath to be homogenous. Thereafter, the mixture containing CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) was extruded in the form of droplets into 200 mL of a crosslinker solution (FeCl3·6H2O, 5 %) using a plastic syringe (40 mL). The distance between the top of the crosslinker solution and the needle tip was fixed at 15 cm. The formed spherical CMS@(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) hydrogel beads were allowed to more crosslink and solidify the core without stirring at 25 °C for 3 h. In the following the unreacted crosslinker (Fe3+ ions) which remained on the surface of the formed beads was removed via rinsing of the formed beads with distilled water. At the next step, the CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) were immersed in the CS solution with slight shaking for 8 h, and the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads were formed. The resulting CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads were rinsed with distilled water to remove extra unreacted CS and then dried in a vacuum for further use.

The NAP content in the hydrogel beads was explored by the digestion method [50]. In a brief, about 10 mg of the sample was pulverized and placed in 20 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Afterward, the mixture was sonicated for 15 min and shaken for 24 h at room temperature. The mixture was filtered and the absorbance of the supernatant was determined via UV–vis spectrophotometer. Finally, the standard calibration curve, Eq. (1), and Eq. (2) obtained the content of NAP in CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)).

The in vitro degradation of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) was evaluated in lysozyme solution. For this regard, certain weights of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) were immersed in centrifuge tubes containing PBS (pH 7.4, lysozyme (13 μg/mL)) at 37 °C. The mixture was shaken on a shaker. At each definite time, the mixtures were decanted, rinsed, dried, and finally weighed. Finally, the mass loss (%) was calculated using Eq. (3).
(3)
M a s s l o s s
%
=
(
M 0 - M t
M 0
)
× 100
In Eq. (3), the M0 and Mt respectively are the original and remaining mass of each centrifuge tube containing the sample at a time “0″ and “t”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the swelling behavior of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) core–shell hydrogel beads
ce:section.ce:para:
The swelling behavior of the prepared NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)/CMS core–shell hydrogel beads was investigated by a gravimetric method in a simulated gastrointestinal (GI) tract condition. Typically the beads were dried in an oven at 40 °C to a constant weight and then a certain weight of the dried beads was immersed in 30 mL of the buffer solutions with definite pH. At a set moment (as shown in Fig. 8b), the samples were taken out, their surface moisture was removed carefully with filter paper, and afterward, the weight of swelled beads was recorded. The swelling percentage of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) hydrogel beads was calculated by the following Eq. (4):
(4)
Swelling
( % )
=
( W s - W d )
Ws
x 100
Where Ws (g) and Wd (g) respectively are the weight of swollen beads at time t and the initial weight of dry beads.

To evaluate and compare the in vitro NAP release pattern from NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) hydrogel beads, about 50 mg of the samples were immersed in buffer solution with pH 1.2 (simulated gastric fluid, SGF) for 2 h. Afterward, the samples were transferred to buffer solution with pH 6.8 (simulated first zone of intestinal fluid, SIF) for 2 h, and finally moved to buffer solution with pH 7.4 (simulated colonic fluid, SCF) for a 4 h. In the definite time intervals, approximately 3 mL of the release medium was removed for adsorption analysis and it was replaced with the same volume of the fresh buffer solution. In the overall release experiment, the temperature of the release medium was adjusted to 37 °C, and shaking was performed with a constant speed to ensure that most of the loaded NAP was extracted by the used buffer. The absorbance of the released NAP into the buffer solution was detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at the maximum wavelength of NAP, which corresponded to the released NAP. In the end, the percentage of the released NAP from the samples was determined using the absorbance of the solution, standard calibration, volume of release medium, and Eq. (5).
(5)
N A P r e l e a s e
% w
=
AmountofreleasedNAP
AmountofloadedNAP
× 100

A cytotoxicity assay was performed to evaluate the biocompatibility of the samples, as well as the effect of CMS and CS on the biocompatibility of the final system. Usually, the HT 29 cells at a density of 104 cells per well were seeded in the 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The cultured cells in the 96-well plates were treated with the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) samples and then the plate was incubated for 8 h at 37 °C. The cells without treatment were considered as control. After this 8 h, the unconsumed culture media was eliminated and 200 μL of the MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. The cells were incubated for another 4 h at 37 °C. Thereafter the unreacted MTT was removed and 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well. The 96-well plates were shaken for 15 min to dissolve the appeared purple-blue crystals of MTT formazan. The cell viability (%) was specified by recording the absorbance of the solution at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm, and administering the ELISA plate reader.

The antibacterial performance of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads was examined via determination of the bacterial minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of it to validate its antibacterial performance against two bacteria; gram-negative Escherichia coli and gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus. Typically, a fresh culture of the bacteria was prepared in a Muller-Hinton culture medium and then diluted to achieve a cell density of 107 colony-forming units (CFU) per mL in a 96-well plate. On the other hand, 100 µL of different concentrations of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) (300 µg/mL, 150 µg/mL, 75 µg/mL, 37.5 µg/mL, 18.75 µg/mL, 9.37 µg/mL) were prepared, sterilized, and added to each well. A positive control group (bacterial and culture medium) was also considered. After 24 h, MICs were determined as the lowest concentration of sample that lead to no visible bacterial growth [51].

Today the design and development of the new more effective multifunctional pH-controlled poorly water-soluble drug delivery systems have remained challenging. Although, to date, a wide range of systems was synthesized, however, for the first time, in this work, photoluminescent GQDs having numerous carboxylic acid functional groups were prepared via pyrolysis of CA. In the following, these surface groups acted as the sites for the growth of porous Bio-MOF(Cu) via the coordination of metal ions of Bio-MOF(Cu) on them. Relay these advantages, the NAP was loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) via several interactions like trapping in porous sites, π-π staking, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding. In the following, the NAP release was explored for NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu). Because of the fast drug release at simulated stomach conditions and considering that the main aim of the current research is the controlled drug releases in colon conditions, the surface of NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was coated with CMS@CS biopolymer with a special structure to induce the pH-sensitivity, controlled and targeted delivery to the final system. Additionally, it is essential to mention that all used regents were biomaterial and not harmful to the human body which the following was supported by the results of the MTT test. Fig. 1 shows the strategy involved in the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) synthesis. The structure of the products was confirmed by the common characterization techniques. Finally, the applicability of the prepared system was explored as a colon-specific drug delivery system.

The structure of the products was studied by FT-IR spectroscopy to further investigate the functional groups present in their structure. As shown in Fig. 2 a the GQDs presented the main peaks related to the stretching vibration of –OH, stretching vibration of –CH, asymmetric stretching vibration of –COO– and also CC (as a merged), stretching vibration of CO, and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COO– respectively at 3248 cm−1, 3031 cm−1, 1600–1800 cm−1, 1122 cm−1, and 1393 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum of Bio-MOF(Cu) the peaks at 2600–3600 cm−1 can be assigned as the hydrogen-bonded NH stretching vibration and –OH stretching vibration. Additionally, the detected peaks at 1560 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 respectively are related to asymmetric stretching and symmetric stretching vibration of –COO– indicating that the carboxylate groups are coordinated to the metal ion in Bio-MOF(Cu) structure [52]. In addition, low-intensity bands of the M−O and M−N stretching vibrations were observed near 500–700 cm−1. Most of the characteristic bands of GQDs were observed clearly in GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) spectrum. Also, the detected partial shift to a higher wavenumber indicates the anchoring of Bio-MOF(Cu) onto the surface of GQDs through the coordinate interaction of Bio-MOF(Cu). The loading of NAP by GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) can be approved by increasing the peak intensity of the CH groups at 2932 cm−1, as well as, the appearance of the band at around 1453 cm−1 (due to the NAP aromatic ring). CMS possesses the broads at 3419 cm−1, 2927 cm−1, 1602 cm−1, 1421 cm−1, and 1016–1095 cm−1 because occurring the stretching vibration of the –OH, the stretching vibrations of the CH, and asymmetric stretching vibration of –COO–, symmetric stretching vibration of –COO–, and sugar ring absorption. Crosslinked CMS showed all of the main peaks corresponding to both constituents, CMS and NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu). Also, compared with CMS the CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) presented a peak at 592 cm−1 with an enhanced intensity that could be assigned to the stretching vibration of Fe-O as a result of cross-linking. The pristine CS presented the matched peaks at 3400–3600 cm−1, 2875 cm−1, 1639 cm−1, 1606 cm−1, 1295–1427 cm−1, and 1000–1290 cm−1 respectively related to intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonds, stretching vibration of the –CH, CO of acetamide, bending vibration of the NH, stretching vibration of the CN, and stretching vibration of the CO. The formed polyelectrolyte complex demonstrates a spectrum similar to that of CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) and CS which indicates the presence of both CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) and CS in its structure. Moreover, the band related to the CH stretching (∼2928 cm−1) became more intense likely because of the CH2 groups of CS and the hydroxyl signal peaks observed in the FT-IR spectra of CMS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) at 3447 cm−1 moved to a lower frequency of 3434 cm−1, suggesting hydrogen bonding [53]. Taken together, these finds suggest the successful synthesis of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), NAP loading on it, and finally the presence of CMS@CS in the carrier backbone.

The crystal phase structure and purity of the prepared samples were examined by the XRD technique. The XRD pattern of the GQDs exhibited the dominant broad peak at 2θ ∼ 24° corresponding to its hexagonal lattice structure, attributed to the (002) peak (JCPDS Card No, 75–0444) [54,55]. Interlayer spacing of GQDs obtained about 3.7 Å via the Bragg equation. The obtained value of the GQDs interlayer spacing is similar to the graphitic materials interlayer spacing and can approve the GQDs formation through the CA pyrolysis. As observed in Fig. 3 , Bio-MOF(Cu) presented the main peaks located at 2θ 10-12°, 2θ 16-20°, and 2θ 21-24° in its XRD analysis, which was in accordance with the available JCPDS cards [21–1599] and reported studies [46,52]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the Bio-MOF(Cu) was successfully synthesized via a facile room-temperature method [46]. Meanwhile, for the synthesized Bio-MOF(Cu) in the presence of GQDs, as well as the drug-loaded system the XRD patterns were consistent with those of the Bio-MOF(Cu) indicating that the GQDs did not change the structure of the substance. This outcome is consistent with the findings reported in the literature [56]. The XRD pattern of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) presents the characteristic diffraction peak of amorph materials at 2θ values of 18-26° which can be attributed to the formation of the crosslinking points in its structure. Moreover, this spectrum shows some of the main peaks of NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) with low intensity. Overall these findings confirmed the successful formation of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) beads and are well-matched with FT-IR output.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was achieved at −196 °C to obtain further evidence about its porous networks. The achieved BET, N2 isotherms, and BJH diagrams are displayed in Fig. 4 . The measured related textural data; pore diameter, pore volume, and surface area of GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm were respectively about 9.55 nm, 0.00611 cm3/g, and 2.56 m2/g. Because the obtained value for pore diameter was larger than NAP size, the dimensions of the NAP molecules (9.7 Å×3.7 Å) it can be concluded that the NAP molecules can be loaded in the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) cavity. This observation approves that the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) is able to load the drug molecules not only through anchoring onto the surface but also via insertion of their in ints pore cavity as previously shown via FT-IR analysis.

The photoluminescent characteristics of the prepared samples, GQDs, GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) were studied at room temperature and the achieved spectra are shown in Fig. 5 . From Fig. 5, it can be seen that the yellowish GQDs under normal daylight can emit blue light after excitation under 365 nm UV excitation. In comparison with net GQDs, both GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) and CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) showed the typical broad emission bands with the lowest intensity after excitation at 365 nm, and the positions of the main peaks were shifted to lower wavelength. The results indicate that the luminescent intensity can be reduced by Bio-MOF(Cu) growth and coating with biopolymers as a result of the filter effect, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, and partial quenching. A splitting pattern was also observed in these spectra due to phenomena such as intersystem transitions, internal and external transformations, and self-absorbing [57]. However, the obtained results can support the suitability of the prepared system for bioimaging with an acceptable photoluminescent feature. So, a survey of the published works approves the suitability of the system with a PL intensity in this range for bioimaging applications [58,59].

The structure and morphological features of the synthesized samples were inspected by the electron microscopy technique. Instrumentally SEM analysis (Fig. 6 ) revealed that the synthesized GQDs are well dispersed with a uniform diameter in the range of 6–7 nm, determined via Image J software. The SEM micrograph of Bio-MOF(Cu) displayed its crystalline structure with a diameter of around 1 μm [47]. In comparison with Bio-MOF(Cu), the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) showed a different surface morphology. So, the GQDs decorated on the Bio-MOF(Cu) can be observed on the surface, suggesting the successful formation of Bio-MOF(Cu) in the presence of GQDs. In Fig. 6c the red arrows were indexed as GQDs. In the following the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) morphology was assessed in two different magnifications. The CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads were spherical. As well as it was observed that the obtained ternary sample of CMS, CS, and NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) are wrapped together and formed a rough surface that expresses a network-like porous nanostructure. The observed roughness can be ascribed as a suitable character for the prepared drug delivery system.

Further evidence about the morphology of the synthesized GQDs and GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was obtained via AFM analyses (Fig. 7
). The finding of this test shows that the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) has more average height, in contrast to that of the pristine GQDs. So, the emergence of the higher height profile could be attributed to the growth of Bio-MOF(Cu) in the presence of GQDs. It should be noticed that the enhanced surface roughness potentially is suitable for drug loading which is a good criterion for each drug carrier.

The biodegradability of each drug delivery system is one of the beneficial features, so these types of systems can degrade after the drug release process. For this reason, the change in weight of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) after dispersion in lysozyme solution was examined at specific times to assess its in vitro biodegradability potential. Fig. 8a shows the obtained plot after 3 days of incubation in lysozyme. Accordingly, about 68.7 % of degradation percentages were achieved after 72 h incubation of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) in the enzymatic medium at 37 °C. Lysozyme can interact with both CMS and CS and ease the hydrolysis of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) via hydrolysis of the glycosidic linkages. The results revealed that CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) is biodegradable in the presence of lysozyme as a result of the hydrophilic properties of the native biopolymers and their biodegradable nature. Therefore CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) can be proposed as a biodegradable NAP delivery agent.

Swelling is one of the main features that determine the release behavior of the loaded drug in the swellable structures. Therefore, the uptake of buffer into CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) was evaluated in simulated GI tract transit to evaluate its potential as an oral delivery system (Fig. 8b). The obtained results showed that after 2 h in H 1.2, a partial penetration of buffer occurred (145.7 %). While, when the system was transferred to pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 the system became more hydrated (995.0–1760.5 %) and its structures were puffed. These might be related to the fact that in acidic conditions, the carboxylate groups of the coating layer are converted to carboxyl groups (–COOH), and a rigid system is obtained because of the hydrogen bond formation. In addition, only a few CS amine groups were protonated in this medium, indicating that the CMS as the matrix played the main role in the swelling. While the deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups in pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 turns the –COOH groups into –COO-, the formed electrostatic repulsion accelerates the buffer penetration, and swelling occurred. Moreover, the Fe ions are displaced with phosphate ions, leading to higher electrostatic repulsion between these charged groups. This then leads to an enhancement in buffer penetrating and an increase in the swelling percentage. Based on these results, the prepared CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) could be proposed for a sustained oral colon drug delivery vehicle [60].

Drug load and release pattern are the crucial factors that determine the efficiency of each newly introduced drug delivery system. In the current study, the NAP was loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) by suspending it in a NAP drug solution. The NAP loading capacity and entrapment efficiency respectively were obtained at around 5.31 % and 53.1 %. The π-π interaction between the benzene ring of NAP and the GQDs, as well as the trapping in the GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) porous sites, are the possible interactions. Moreover, the drug can be loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) via electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond formation between the polar groups of NAP and GQDs@Bio-MOF [61–63].

In the following, the in vitro NAP release from NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) was tested in the simulated GI tract at 37 °C; (pH 1.2, 2 h), (pH 6.8, 2 h), and (pH 7.4, 2 h), which simulate respectively the pH of the gastric fluid, the first intestinal fluid, the fluid of the second intestinal region. As can be seen the NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) showed relatively a burst NAP release, so, 47.84 % of the loaded drug was released in 2 h. To solve this limitation, the CMS and CS biopolymers were used as a green coat for NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) to protect it and obtain an oral drug system with sustained and controlled drug release ability. The NAP loading capacity and entrapment efficiency of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) respectively were obtained at around 1.26 % and 12.6 %. A study of the NAP release profile from the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) showed that the drug release from it is sustained and in a controlled manner which are good criteria for each oral drug delivery system. So the percentage of the NAP release drugs was obtained at about 16.04 % in simulated stomach conditions and more releases occurred in simulated medium with colon conditions, 71.58 % of release at pH 6.8 and pH 7.4. These findings can approve the suitability of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) for colon-specific delivery and are in the same line with [64–66]. The obtained release patterns for both systems are shown in Fig. 8c. A comparison of the charts approved the improvement in drug release patterns which can be attributed to the special feature of the fabricated polysaccharide-based coat. In fact the pH-sensitive nature of the coat limits its expansion in the acidic media. However, the system expands in the alkaline condition and this facilitates the buffer diffusion and leads to more drug release which is in accordance with swelling results. The proposed oral NAP delivery process for CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) is provided in Fig. 8d. In the simulated pH of the stomach condition, the protonation of carboxyl in an acidic environment and more crosslinking restrict the buffer diffusion and hence NAP release. However, when the system moved to a basic environment, the release rate was enhanced because of the deportation of the carboxylic groups, expansion of the network, more buffer diffusion, and finally more NAP releases. The outcome revealed that the coating of NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu) with CMS@CS results in a sustained and controlled drug release and warrants the suitability of the fabricated CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) for an excellent oral delivery to the colon.

MTT test was performed against HT 29 cell line as a model of the human colon cell line to consider the cytocompatibility of the prepared systems. Fig. 9 shows the cell viability of the samples in the selected concentrations range. The obtained results indicate that the cell viability for the treated cells with CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) in the selected concentrations (0 to 60 µg·mL−1) is higher than NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu), 78.3 % and 65.7 % respectively for CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) and NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu). This acceptable cytocompatible nature of the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) can be related to the nonsignificant cytotoxicity nature of the used coat, physically crosslinked CMS, and CS. The finding is in good accordance with the previously reported studies [67] and can prove the cytocompatibility of the prepared system as an oral delivery system. Overall from the obtained results, the constructed CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) could potentially recommend as a new safe oral drug delivery system without any concerns about its cytotoxicity which results from the presence of non-toxic CMS and CS in its structure.

In recent days microbes with antibiotic-resistant are speedily spreading and the risks caused by them remain difficult. Consequently, the preparation of a new drug carrier with inherent antimicrobial properties is very valuable. By considering these, the antibacterial activity of CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) was evaluated on standard strains of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, two major classes of bacteria. The MIC value for both Staphylococcus aureus Escherichia coli obtained was at about 75 µg·mL−1 via visual detection. These findings showed that the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) have antibacterial activity against both studied bacteria. This antibacterial activity may be is due to the combinational effect of the Cu-based Bio-MOF and CS coat in the structure. Therefore, it was found that the prepared CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads are suitable for use in biomedicinal applications with potent antimicrobial activities to prevent the growth of organisms.

In summary, in this work, the CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) was developed as a new pH-controlled oral NAP delivery system with a sustained NAP release pattern. In this regard, at first, the Bio-MOF(Cu) was synthesized in the presence of pre-synthesized GQDs. Then about 53.01 % of NAP was successfully loaded into fabricated GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu). The drug release rate of NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF was fast at pH 1.2 while when it was capped with CMS@CS the rate of NAP release was decreased at pH 1.2, while the release rate was increased at pH 6.8 and 7.4. The synthesized samples were characterized using several analysis tools. The obtained swelling profiles approved the pH-sensitivity of the developed system, the highest swelling in alkaline media. The use of only water during the generation of all of the samples is another advantage of this work in which there was no need to use toxic chemicals or solvents. MIC value against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was calculated at about 75 µg mL−1 which substantiates that it can precisely transport the drug into colon cells without any concerns about bacterial infections. Besides, formulated CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) beads illustrated no notable cellular toxicity. As well as the performed degradation study, proved their biodegradation under physiological conditions, 68.7 % after 72 h. However, this system still focuses on in vitro drug delivery, in vivo studies are necessary in the near future to o confirm its performance with full confidence. Overall, these findings emphasize that the synthesized biodegradable CMS@CS/(NAP@GQDs@Bio-MOF(Cu)) could be used as a sustained and controlled oral delivery agent with more release of NAP under simulated colon conditions without any cytotoxicity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, the Tabriz University of Medical Science for their financial supports of this research.
